Can you tell me a little bit more about your use case for this? Chalice can publish a large set of resources including multiple lambda functions an an API gateway REST API, as well as possibly event sources such as CloudWatch Events. The main use case I can see for this is rolling back your application, which is very useful since it would only roll back the lambda component(s) of the Chalice app. The app's resources would then possibly be out of sync, APIs in API Gateway that go nowhere etc... This is only going to get worse as we add more resource types to Chalice through #516.

The correct way at the moment to version your application would be to roll forward by running `chalice deploy` again, which will ensure all your resources are in sync. You can use your version control system to make tags for certain known good releases and you can simply check them out and redeploy to deploy an older version of the app.

Let me put here another experience, that may help implementing this: don't give the user direct access to the array, but rather with a function getValue(), as several other libs (e.g. Nio, ScientificNetcdf). I even think there is a way in python of turning a slice [:] into a function call, I believe this is what NetCDF4 and h5py are doing, but I'm not sure we should. 

Beside of only reading variables when needed, this had one unexpected advantage that I already used once: by manipulating that getValue() I was able of add pseudo-variables, i.e. a variables that look just like the others but is not present in the file (nor in the memory). Once needed its calculated from the others (e.g. KDP from PHIDP), moreover parameter of this calculus can be changes at any time. I'm not saying at all that this kind of manipulation is a good programing idea, but it has really helpful once.

At last, there are also some negative points with getValue(), first backwards compatibility. Second we would not be able to close the file in the end of read(), but rather keep the open file within Radar. That may have some performance consequences that I do not fully understand. Third if not implemented correctly it may cause Radar behavior to be dependent of the original file type (mdv, netcdf, sigmet etc.), we definitely don't want that. 

P.S. I fully agree using NetCDF4, as it is the official lib, but are you aware it is some folds slower then its concurrents? including scipy.io, ScientificNetcdf, Nio and h5py.

I agree with Ines' distinction between "reconstruction" and "condition-specific model derived from a reconstruction". @cdanielmachado , I don't think Ines says that a reconstruction is *not* a model, but that a reconstruction is a more general model.

Let me define my usage of terms before continuing:

* (Metabolic) **reconstruction** = list of biochemical reactions occurring in some organism of interest, identifying participating metabolites and enzymes, proteins and genes etc. It is a "reconstruction" in that the network is not directly observable but must be pieced together from available evidence. Thus, it may not cover *everything*, may telescope intermediate reactions, etc.

* **Model** is a more general term, denoting a purposeful simplification of reality, designed to imitate certain phenomena (e.g. metabolism) or characteristics of a system (e.g. yeast) while downplaying non-essential aspects. Its value lies in the ability to generalise insights from the model to a broader class of related systems. Thus, a lab mouse can be a model representing mammals in general; an in vitro heart cell can represent the cells in an intact heart; and a set of differential equations can approximate the dynamic behaviour of a biological system.

I think Memote should validate both recons and condition-specific models *as far as applicable*. So a recon can be tested for quality of annotation, dead ends, etc., whereas other tests may require the specification of a medium, condition-specific biomass compositions, expressed genes, etc. In any case, it is vital that condition-specific models can be traced back to their parent recon.

Furthermore, I agree that it is necessary to distinguish between the medium/environment and the system/cell within it (cf the mention of .yml config files in the section "Data-dependent tests"). Simulating different media serves as [virtual experiments]( https://peerj.com/preprints/273/) that characterize the model of the system.

This has implications for standards requirements for GEMs. I realize now that [MIRIAM](http://dx.doi.org/10.1038/nbt1156) (Box 3, point 5) does not address flux balance models, only full-fledged ordinary differential equations (my emphasis):

> The encoded model **must be instantiated** in a simulation. This means that quantitative attributes of the model have to be defined. Therefore, the model must contain, or be associated with, values (or ranges of values) for **all initial conditions** and parameters, as well as **kinetic expressions for all reactions**. These values can be provided as a separate file from the model itself.

This means that we cannot claim MIRIAM compliance as per the le Novère et al. 2005 paper, even though we can and should demand MIRIAM-compliant annotations (table 1 of the Memote draft).

While flux balance models draw much of their strength from *not* requiring kinetic expressions, the wish to specify an external medium is similar to MIRIAM's requirement for initial conditions and parameter estimates. The same goes for biomass composition, which is specific to the organism's "mode of operation", e.g. growth rate, and tissue-specific in multicellular organisms. (This is also why I think it's important to distinguish "objective function" from "biomass composition".)

I agree that the test for blocked reactions all is rather strict by accepting no blocked reactions at all. I wouldn't want to reduce it to a mere warning though, because of those cases where it might be problems with gap filling. However, arbitrarily relaxing the test requirements doesn't strike me as a good idea either.

There's got to be a more informed way to set a threshold. Perhaps some extrapolation depending on the genome annotations, or an average value of the portion of blocked reactions across models in ratio to the total amount of reactions?

OK, I didn't realize that username/password are never used as the basic auth header. I'll look into making that change. As for the `client_id` I may need some more explanation. You'll see in the [original issue I had raised on DOT](https://github.com/evonove/django-oauth-toolkit/issues/353) that was what I was trying to get working.

So when using the password grant type (Resource Owner Password Credentials Grant) and a public client, the `client_secret` isn't sent along. In this grant type you're authenticating the client and the user separately. If the client is a public client, you really can't make a very strong assertion that the request is authentic and the assumption is that the user should enter their credentials into the app because they trust it (for example it was downloaded from your website or the iOS app store under your name).

The [wording is a bit odd](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-2.3.1) in that it's required but only if it's not blank:

    client_secret
         REQUIRED.  The client secret.  The client MAY omit the
         parameter if the client secret is an empty string.

It also is allowed to be blank in the DOT `Application` object. I was planning on opening a DOT issue regarding this because there's some assumptions built into it that kind of make it not work very cleanly. This issue came up for me while testing some code related to that.
On the other hand, I'd argue that all of OAuth has a documentation issue.

tl;dr: It's working, but via an unofficially supported mechanism, and so it needs to be fixed, which I can do.

Longer story:

These are for the Python standard logging handlers, which is not the main API interface we document, it's just a helper that I added. I documented  it under "Additional tools" in the Cloud docs and a section at the bottom of these Github docs.

As mentioned, on App Engine Flex, stdout/stderr ends up in Stackdriver Logging, but the reason I just don't like logging to that (which is where Python standard logging goes by default), is because the fluentd logger doesn't capture error severity levels. So if you try to search for messages logged at severity `error` the best you can do is a text search for the word `error`, and you will get all message that have the word error in them regardless of their log level. It's a small detail but I think a nice one to get right. So the current handler tries to format the messages so the fluentd agent parses the severity correctly. Justin is telling me that I shouldn't rely on the fluentd agent at all and should just write directly to the API.

The only small details to doing that right is to correctly set the monitored resource so the logs show up in the correct App Engine service/version logs rather than in custom global logs. 

I can submit a PR to fix it, currently a few things ahead in my queue, maybe this week but if not then after Next is over (next week). @JustinBeckwith can let me know if it's higher priority than I'm realizing.

What I meant by "parses the buffer" is that python holds a memory buffer for what gets typed in the interpreter… and that can be accessed, and parsed… and `dill` does that.  See almost everything in `dill.source`  (https://github.com/uqfoundation/dill/blob/master/dill/source.py)… for example, `dill.source.findsource`.  For objects defined in `__main__`, `__main__` is treated much like it were a module, and basically, this "file" is also read from the buffer… this is also important when searching for the global object references for functions… as is done in `dill.dill.save_function` (see here: https://github.com/uqfoundation/dill/blob/master/dill/dill.py#L776, and subsequently, `dill.detect.globalvars`.

@stringertheory I am developing a library [py-stl](github.com/mvcisback/py-stl) for working within Metric Temporal Logic and Signal Temporal Logic. 

These are often used to determine if a time series signal meets some specification (for filtering perhaps). For example, one may write `G[0, 10]((x > 0) -> F[0, 1](y < -1))` which translates to:
> Between time 0 and 10, if x is greater than zero, then within the next 1 time steps y will be less than negative one.

My library would take the two signals `x(t)` and `y(t)` and evaluate the above formula. The problem is if `x` and `y` are defined on different domains, then the result may not be well defined.

For example, if `x.domain = (0, 10)`, but `y.domain = (0, 4)`, then the above formula is only defined if for all t > 4, x(t) = False.

It's worth noting that I made the design decision to make `x` and `y` different `TimeSeries` objects since it means I can often compress `x > 0` and `y < -1` quite abit using the `compact` method. This makes evaluation much more efficent, particularly if one is interested in at what time points a particular formula is `True` (similar to doing a sliding window and evaluating the formula on the window).

I believe this is not strictly related to this project, the integration of behave and Django.

Behave allows you to create XML reports in junit format, e.g. in your [behave configuration file](http://behave.readthedocs.io/en/latest/behave.html?highlight=junit#configuration-files) add:
``` ini
# -- your behave configuration file (.behaverc, behave.ini, setup.cfg)
[behave]
junit = yes
junit_directory = tests/reports
```
Then the rest of the integration with Jenkins should just work [as usual with jUnit](https://wiki.jenkins-ci.org/display/JENKINS/JUnit+Plugin).

It has been a while, but someone else may need this information in future.

As mentioned in previous comment, docx.table._Cell object has property _tc which contains useful information about cell's span:

```
from docx import Document
doc=Document("simple.docx")
table=doc.tables[0]

c=table.cell(0,0)
print(c.text, c._tc.top, c._tc.bottom, c._tc.left, c._tc.right)

c=table.cell(0,1)
print(c.text, c._tc.top, c._tc.bottom, c._tc.left, c._tc.right)

c=table.cell(1,1)
print(c.text, c._tc.top, c._tc.bottom, c._tc.left, c._tc.right)

```
The output will be:
```
a 0 2 0 1
b 0 1 1 2
c 1 2 1 2
```

From this you will easily understand not only if the cell is merged or not, but also its shape and size.

With regard to the objects, we still had to figure out how to link the  two parts of a Bookmark (bookmarkStart and bookmarkEnd) together, if there is like a paragraph in between.

My suggestion would be to make two ways to add a bookmark;
```python
# This adds a paragraph containing the start and stop of the bookmark
document.add_bookmark(name='test', inplace=True)

# This places a bookmark start:
bookmark_obj_1 = document.add_bookmark(name='test_1')
bookmark_obj_1.start_bookmark()

# Start some new run, with text which maybe contains another bookmark:
par = document.add_paragraph()
bookmark_obj_2 = par.add_bookmark(name'test_2')
bookmark.start_bookmark()
# In this case, the bookmark contains the text, which could be referred to
par.add_run('sometext')
bookmark_obj_2.end_bookmark()

# Add the end of the first bookmark:
bookmark_obj_1.end_bookmark()

```
This does mean that one has to correctly place the bookmark object to be able to succesfully add the end the bookmark. I'm not entirely sure about the exact implementation.
I think it has some challenges;
- The bookmark object needs to 'know' in which paragraph or run it needs to be placed.
- The user needs to keep track of the exact object which contains the info about the specific bookmark. It might be more elegant to be able to end the bookmark by calling `document.end_bookmark(name='test')` and this places the corresponding `<w:bookmarkEnd>` element.

crazily, the reason things were breaking was [this change](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri-installer-windows/compare/v1.1.2...v1.2.0#diff-03d5f197d7e34c30938a1baf6746d5daL187): switching from `http://127.0.0.1:8080/learn/#!/learn` to `http://127.0.0.1:8080/`

* It turned out that because the old URL included stuff after the `#` (which a browser normally wouldn't send), the server was responding with a `404`
* After we switched the new URL, the server responded with a `302` and activated middleware which hit the browser by creating a session

In both cases, we were polling to see if the server was up. But after fixing the URL, it actually started weighing down the server enough to cause the DB locking.

@indirectlylit 
> @benjaoming you mentioned:
> 
> >  I believe we need an explicit, deliberate, and unambiguous version that's kept in the code (as data in our git repo)
> 
> It's a totally fair perspective, but I guess I don't understand why the need exists?

It's because git tags are made in a certain way and with the previous "beta5 by mistake" unnoticed after tagging beta6, 7, 8 and 9, I wouldn't feel comfortable about relying on tags for production releases at the very least.

I'm open about relaxing the assertions that are introduced to verify consistency between `kolibri.VERSION` and tags/VERSION files for pre-releases. But IMO this has to be done once an alternative to `git describe` is in place and tested.

Re: some other things....

> **When a user reports a bug with some version info, we should be able to unambiguously map the version number to a particular commit hash.**

This is kept in place in the automation of version suffixes for pre-releases.

>  To avoid multiple sources of truth. (...)

Agreed, I try to achieve it with assertions and exceptions that will inevitably show up and avoid building/releasing anything :)

> To avoid git commits in our repo like “updated version number”:
> 
>   * tend to cause issues e.g. when synced from release branches back to develop
>   * needing to re-tag just because of version-change commit

These are nice commits IMO :) They clarify in the git history what's going on. I never had problems with version bumping commits for things that are released, and I like the conflicts that arise.

You also get the benefit of being able to isolate a version bump into a PR:

```
git checkout -b "new-pre-release"
# edit VERSION in kolibri/__init__.py
git add kolibri/__init__.py
git commit -m "Bumping version"
git push origin
# open pull request
```

Now, what happens in the PR is that `kolibri.VERSION` is newer than any of the tags, which is allowed! In the PR, the builds are called `0.5b10` with no suffixing.

If the PR is acceptable, someone merges the PR to `develop` and then tag the commit, which will initiate a base for automated version suffixing.

I think with all this said, that if we consider 2 use cases, they are both satisfied:

1. Rapid and automated releasing of test builds with clear identification of commit hashes etc.
1. Deliberate releasing of public final and pre-releases. If there are merge conflicts here, they are welcomed and should be resolved knowingly.

>  For (1), without git information we really know nothing about the state of the code so it might be misleading to "fall back" on a version file. Perhaps for these cases, we should just build with version 0.0.0 or something similarly ambiguous?

Well, all distributed code is outside the scope of git information :) My first intuition about all this was the craze of spawning a `git` subprocess every time we even load `kolibri.__init__` just to see if Git information was available.. IMO, this is not suitable for production, so I started by isolating final releases from this behaviour... and for that, you need `kolibri.VERSION` or similar.
The self-review is helpful. recommend moving those to actual comments in the code for posterity.

Hello Himanshu,

I will not add this feature to Scout2 because the tool is not meant to do any sort of parsing/analysis of the activity logs. This warning can easily be set by accessing the credentials report, but access to the activity requires downloading and parsing CloudTrail log files.

I have done this before using different tools. If you already send and aggregate your cloudtrail logs somewhere, you can probably easily query this information there. Otherwise, you can try to do it on your computer assuming you have a decent amount of available disk space & your account does not create an insanely big amount of logs.

You can download all the CloudTrail log files using this python tool from the AWS-recipes repo:
https://github.com/nccgroup/AWS-recipes/blob/master/Python/aws_cloudtrail_get_logs.py

Then write your own python code that does something like:
for each file in logs, open it, transform the JSON string to a python object, iterate through all events and save/print those associated with the root account.

The "unreadable ternseness" derives not from assembling XPath via Python `%` string formatting but rather it is that the reader is expected to understand, without context or comment, that `'List of Un'` means the code is excluding the list of unpublished decisions from the set of opinion PDFs. I don't think most readers would understand that Un was short for Unpublished or cotton to the significance. Hence...unreadable terseness.


That said, I do think the `%` assebly of XPath is confusing (but not terse) because it requires following a bunch of layers of indirection and whiplashing between lines 72, 73, 66, 74, 67, 75, 68-70, to read and understand the code. But I'm not proposing a change to that.

> Commands that outputting a singular output value shouldn't be allowing --format XXX options at all.

They definitely need to support `--format JSON` -- that's how we provide a uniform machine-parseable format. And once they support `--format JSON`, then you need a descriptor for non-JSON output so that you can append a respecification of format to a prior command.

I feel very strongly about all commands which are at all capable supporting JSON output. We may want exceptions in rare cases like `globus login`, as streaming JSON output is messy.

> I generally don't like the ascii-art table format output, because for many commands, unless I have my terminal window set very wide, it does weird wrapping.

I totally get this. I don't really love the wide format of the tables either, and they're terrible when they line wrap. I did consider having a hard wrap at 80 characters, as it's much friendlier to small terminals, but solving the problem of how to present tabular data at a fixed width was not on the docket early in development.

If we're talking about supporting "record-oriented" output for tabular data, that makes sense as a possible solution to this problem.

> I find the two different output formats jarring. I understand there is a logic to it, but it just seems really inconsistent to me.
> ...
> I don't feel super-strongly about having the default output always be one or the other -- if others like a default that some commands are tables, and some are records, I'm ok with that.

At first glance, these feel like they're in conflict with one another.
It's sounding to me like you're comfortable with the current outputs (excepting the open issues to the contrary), but are feeling hampered by the fact that tabular data is _always_ in the ASCII-art table.
Is that an accurate, if brief, summary?

---

As soon as we provide a formatting option like `--format RECORD`, we're implying a stable and parseable output format.
That's not all bad, as I'd rather have a textual parseable format which we actually support than letting people try to parse arbitrary text and have it work "most of the time" with no promise of support.

I'm not sure it makes sense to coerce non-tabular data into the table format, however.
If we add a CSV format, being able to get individual bits of data as CSV rows is presumably desirable, and similar enough.

Another point to note is that while it may appear that installing those two packages would get you a usable Unix environment on Windows, that is not the case. It will get you a bash prompt, but it won't be any good to you. To get that you need to install the (assuming new names) `m2-base` meta-package which depends on: `m2-bash`, `m2-bash-completion`, `m2-catgets`, `m2-coreutils`, `m2-dash`, `m2-file`, `m2-filesystem`, `m2-findutils`, `m2-gawk`, `m2-gcc-libs`, `m2-grep`, `m2-gzip`, `m2-inetutils`, `m2-info`, `m2-less`, `m2-mintty`, `m2-launcher-git`, `m2-msys2-runtime`, `m2-ncurses`, `m2-sed`, `m2-time`, `m2-ttyrec`, `m2-tzcode`, `m2-util-linux`, `m2-which`, `m2-libxml2`, `m2-make`, `m2-unzip`, `m2-diffutils`, so any idea that a few of these can be added to the normal packages channel to get us a lot of what we want is incorrect.

This package is actually a meta-package from `MSYS2` (where it is called `msys2-base`), except I added 3 things that conda-build more or less assumes the environment provides (the last 3). I am in mind to make a meta-package in the default channel called `posix-env` (or something, suggestions welcome) that depends on m2-base and also the last 3 so that updating `m2-base` is trivial.

Thanks for taking a look at this @jdhalimi. To retain the one-to-one mapping of configuration parameters from `conda config` and `.condarc`, I think we don't necessarily want to enable/translate `conda config --add channel conda-canary`. Rather, we should just allow the currently supported
```
conda config --add channels defaults --add channels conda-canary
```
to be optionally be given as
```
conda config --add channels conda-canary defaults
```

There was a good proposal presented for this:

* Allow leading slashes to mean root, except when known `/restapi/v1.0` endpoints are used, then prepend `/restapi/v1.0`.

This approach accomplishes two goals:

* Supports expected behavior
* Does not break backward compatibility

Support expected behavior allows us to use the following principles:

POLA - Priciple of Least Astonishment: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_astonishment
DWIM - Do What I Mean: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DWIM

Though I'm not use to Python, I can see from the code of the function `filter_by_count` in `indexlist.py` that as soon as at least one index is rejected because it doesn't match the regex, the variable `exclude` is set to `True`. But this variable is the method's parameter, and as a result the rest of the method effectively behaves as if `exclude` had been set to `True`.

Now I realize I should be able to work around that by first filtering with a `pattern` filter. That way, the `count` filter would not reject any index based on the regex, and the `exclude` parameter should not be overwritten.

I hope it's clearer :).

Our API query language is designed to accept a desired output data structure as a part of the query argument. So the client can request something like

        language_version: python3

```
from __future__ import print_function

import os
import sys

import ruamel.yaml as yaml
from ruamel.yaml.compat import text_type, binary_type
import difflib

d = difflib.Differ()

def load_yaml_guess_indent(stream):
    # load a yaml file guess the indentation, if you use TABs ...
    if isinstance(stream, text_type):
        yaml_str = inp
    elif isinstance(stream, binary_type):
        yaml_str = stream.decode('utf-8')  # most likely, but the Reader checks BOM for this
    else:
        yaml_str = stream.read()
    indent = 2  # default if not found for some reason
    prev_line_key_only = None
    for line in yaml_str.splitlines():
        rline = line.rstrip()
        if rline.startswith('- '):
            idx = 1
            while line[idx] == ' ':  # this will end as we rstripped
                idx += 1
            if line[idx] == '#':     # comment after -
                continue
            indent = idx
            break
        if rline.endswith(':'):
            idx = 0
            while line[idx] == ' ':  # this will end on ':'
                idx += 1
            prev_line_key_only = idx
            continue
        if prev_line_key_only is not None and rline:
            idx = 0
            while line[idx] in ' -':  # this will end on ':'
                idx += 1
            if idx > prev_line_key_only:
                indent = idx - prev_line_key_only
                break
        prev_line_key_only = None
    return yaml.round_trip_load(yaml_str), indent

yaml_str = """\
-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks.git
    sha: cf550fcab3f12015f8676b8278b30e1a5bc10e70
    # foo
    hooks:
    -   id: trailing-whitespace
"""


data, indent = load_yaml_guess_indent(yaml_str)
print('indent', indent)
res = yaml.dump(data, Dumper= yaml.RoundTripDumper, indent=indent)
if res != yaml_str:

for root, directory_names, file_names in os.walk('.'):
    if '.git' in directory_names:
        directory_names.remove('.git')
    for file_name in file_names:
        if file_name.endswith('.yml') or file_name.endswith('.yaml'):
            print('-' * 60)
            full_name = os.path.join(root, file_name)
            with open(full_name) as fp:
                yaml_str = fp.read()
                data, indent = load_yaml_guess_indent(yaml_str)
                print(full_name, 'indent', indent)
                res = yaml.dump(data, Dumper= yaml.RoundTripDumper, indent=indent)
                if res != yaml_str:
                    result = list(d.compare(yaml_str.splitlines(True), res.splitlines(True)))
                    sys.stdout.writelines(result)
                    print('>>>>>>')
                    print(res)

Missing value != empty one.

Here is a story about `Content-Length: 0` with GET request: http://redmine.lighttpd.net/issues/1717

> `/srv` contains site-specific data which is served by this system. This main purpose of specifying this is so that users may find the location of the data files for particular service, and so that services which require a single tree for readonly data, writable data and scripts (such as cgi scripts) can be reasonably placed. Data that is only of interest to a specific user should go in that users’ home directory.

> `/usr/local/share` - The requirements for the contents of this directory are the same as `/usr/share`. The `/usr/share` hierarchy is for all read-only architecture independent data files. Any program or package which contains or requires data that doesn’t need to be modified should store that data in `/usr/share` (or `/usr/local/share`, if installed locally). It is recommended that a subdirectory be used in `/usr/share` for this purpose.

Hi @jorisvandenbossche - afraid I don't have anything particularly helpful to say on this, other than supporting you in the assertion that this looks like a change to notebook. Of course, if it was an intentional change, we will have to do some patching in the nbagg backend. It would be helpful to get some notebook insight on this.

This is something we've thought about in other kernels as well. Until quite recently, the R kernel was drawing SVG, PNG, JPEG and PDF by default - the last especially made big chunks of binary data. We've now dropped JPEG and PDF from the defaults.

However, I don't want to go too far in the direction of only drawing the format the current client wants. I think it's a bad user experience if you have to explicitly think about whether this is an archival notebook or a quick-and-dirty exploration before making a plot. In a sense, the whole point of the notebook is to make a smoother transition from exploration to a more presentable document. So I think we need defaults that work reasonably well for both.

See, the processing pipeline is this: original image -> preprocessed image -> model output. Most importantly, the model only sees the preprocessed image! Hence, loss.backward() gives you the gradient with respect to the **preprocessed image**. self._process_gradient takes this gradient and returns the gradient with respect to the **original image**. If there is no preprocessing then the two are identical.

Instead of introducing a backward-incompatible API change this could be implemented as a new function for the `Match` object. The function could be named `get_meta` and it could receive a boolean argument `allow_duplicates`, that is false by default. So, `match.meta` would be equivalent to `match.get_meta()`, but `match.get_meta(allow_duplicates=True)` could return a list in case of duplications.

This way existing programs are not affected, but you have a way of getting all meta values with the same name. Thought?

> There's no way to prevent the default value from being set.
>
> *Here's how I believe this should work in 2.0:*
>
>    -
>
>    Defaults are only applied to missing entries. Explicit None values
>    would remain None.
>    -
>
>    When partial is True, defaults are *not* applied at all. It makes zero
>    sense for defaults to pop up when you specifially indicate you're working
>    with partial data.
>    -
>
>    A switch named apply_defaults would be available to force defaults for
>    partial data or, conversely, leave them out from whole objects.

I agree that NAT traversal is a low-priority concern, because as long as there is at least one publicly-routable Atlas node that a zonefile-sender can reach, then all Atlas nodes behind all NATs will eventually be able to fetch the zone file (since they'll each pull it from the publicly-routable node).

The problem I'm trying to solve is make it so the network can continue to operate even if all publicly-routable Atlas nodes get shut down (i.e. through DDoS, through misconfiguration, etc.).  I don't expect NAT traversal to be 100% reliable for 100% of peers, but all we need to have work for this is make it so some peers can at least be contacted if there are no publicly-routable peers.

In terms of prioritization, making the network more reliable by pushing zone files to one another and adding code to ban misbehaving and short-lived peers should come first.  Once these are in place, then we can think about adding support for NAT-traversing nodes that e.g. run on your laptop and frequently switch IP addresses and go offline.

Your criticism is totally correct, @sthirugn. But after weighing the pros and cons, I think it's the best solution available. :/

Method `Organization.subscription` should probably be dropped in favor of method `Subscription.search`. Here's what the method signature could look like:

    def search(self, organization, system, activation_key):

You could use this method to find all subscriptions belonging to a particular organization like so:

```python
>>> subscriptions = entities.Subscription.search(
...    organization=entities.Organization(id=5)
... )
>>> type(subscriptions)
list
>>> isinstance(subscriptions[0], entities.Subscription)
True
```

Creating and passing in an object every time you want to call `Subscription.search` is cumbersome. In response, I think that it's reasonable to offer the same shortcut as is offered by `EntityCreateMixin.create`:

```python
>>> subscriptions = entities.Subscription.search(organization=5)
>>> type(subscriptions)
list
>>> isinstance(subscriptions[0], entities.Subscription)
True
```

As you might have figured out, this particular fix depends on #11.

> If we adding that parameter to each method, so can't we move that parameter on some higher level and remove it from signatures at all?

There's not much in the way of layering for the helper methods. They're much simpler than the mixins. Let's say you call `ActivationKey.add_subscriptions`. When you do, this is (roughly) the call stack:

    nailgun.entities.ActivationKey.add_subscriptions (assemble request arguments)
        nailgun.client.put (perform logging, encode JSON)
            requests.put (make actual request)
        nailgun.entities._handle_response (check for HTTP 202, 4XX, 5XX status codes, decode JSON)

I think it's really useful to automatically check for 202 status codes, much like we check for HTTP 4XX and 5XX status codes. So how do we handle them?

As an aside, we can't rely on requests to provide some mechanism for handling 202 status codes. I don't think Requests provides such functionality, and even if it did, Satellite doesn't provide a full URL for monitoring tasks. As a result, the right place to handle 202 status codes is in NailGun.

    def get_version_info(pm):

        plugin_dist_info = pm.list_plugin_distinfo()
        if plugin_dist_info:

I think the problem with any of these `total` functions is that the whole reason for `relativedelta` is that these deltas do not represent a fixed period of time, so `total_months` is actually ambiguous. Even if you just look at the "plural" arguments, what should `relativedelta(years=1, months=2, days=7).total_months()` return? Should it be `14.25`? Should it be `14`? Or `14.4285`? The semantics aren't obvious.

Then of course there is the problem that `relativedelta` is actually a hybrid of "calendar delta" and "partial datetime", so what should `relativedelta(year=2018).total_months()` return? Just 0? What about `relativedelta(day=3, months=4).total_months()`?

I think when #449 and #524 are done, it's *possible* that `calendardelta` can implement a `total_months()` function, or something similar, though I expect a lot of confusion on that front, since `total_seconds()` actually includes the total number of seconds represented by a `timedelta`.

This is related to the `InlinejavascriptRequirement` issue on windows. As the windows consider `stdin`,`stdout` as file descriptors the current `select` module do not allow us to do Non Blocking I/O. In order to overcome this issue I created a commit that used threads. here https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool/pull/419/commits/69fc0fb9790d020bbd9f079b86a36781ae9f3e87. Onn initial testing on conformance test this solution looked fine. But thanks to Janneke, she pointed out a workflow on which this workflow failed. So Now the problem was:
My current patch worked and did gave output.
```
WorkflowException: :1:1: Expression evaluation error:
:1:1: Long-running script killed after 20 seconds: returncode was: -9
:1:1: script was:
:1:1: 01 "use strict";
:1:1: 02 var inputs = {};
:1:1: 03 var self = null;
:1:1: 04 var runtime = {
:1:1: 05     "outdirSize": 1024, 
:1:1: 06     "ram": 1024, 
:1:1: 07     "tmpdirSize": 1024, 
:1:1: 08     "cores": 1, 
:1:1: 09     "tmpdir": "/tmp/tmpgcXGd6", 
:1:1: 10     "outdir": "/tmp/tmpGqSJ21"
:1:1: 11 };
:1:1: 12 (function(){return ((1+1));})()
:1:1: stdout was: 
:1:1: 2
:1:1: stderr was: 

Process finished with exit code 1
```
notice stdout value 2, it is correct so expression is evaluated correctly. The problem is that as I am using threads instead to do this I/O read. on joining them the thread gets blocked on `os.read` statement as it expects input. I tried to find a way to read data without blocking/ way to check if data is in `stdout` and only read if it exists. On windows I can't find a reliable way. `isatty` didn't help. 
There are now two possible solution that i know of. 
Sol1: promote treads to daemon. Dont join them and they will die once program executes completely. But it has a catch, As @tetron told me we try to use same `jsprocess` as  to evaluate multiple `js expression` as process is expensive. So Now if we use daemon threads when evaluating 2nd expression, thread from first expresion are still alive and would capture 2nd process output also. Its solution: use new `js process` for each expression evaluation. 
Sol 2: use process instead of threads. Although there will be minor speed loss we will have better handling over it. So when we think we have received output, we can terminate process. This way we can utilise single `js process`. 
Personally I am inclined to second method. what do you think @mr-c @anton-khodak ,Janneke

I actually found the issue. It is not related to CKeditor but to the way Django handles static files. I have put my custom plugin into the ckeditor folder located in the static folder. This s wrong. The static folder designated by the STATIC_ROOT is to be filled only by running collectstatic. Any file added in other ways will be ignored. By putting the files related to the custom plugin into another folder, listed in STATICFILES_DIRS, then running collectstatic, it gets added to the STATIC_ROOT folder and can then be served. bind-key b split-window

# Ctrl-a as prexix as in screen
set-option -g prefix C-a

# Allows for faster key repetition
set -s escape-time 0

# Allows us to use C-a a <command> to send commands to a TMUX session inside
# another TMUX session
bind-key a send-prefix

# hjkl pane traversal
bind h select-pane -L
bind j select-pane -D
bind k select-pane -U
bind l select-pane -R

bind-key C command-prompt -p "Name of new window: " "new-window -n '%%'"

# reload config
bind r source-file ~/.tmux.conf \; display-message "Config reloaded..."

# Add powerline
run-shell "powerline-daemon -q"
source-file /usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/powerline/bindings/tmux/powerline.conf

set -g default-terminal "screen-256color"

if-shell "[ -f ~/.tmux.local.conf ]" 'source ~/.tmux_local.conf'
```

One solution to that would be to extend the implementation of `_get_json_type_for_field` by adding a loop going through the base field types using `isinstance()` to detect the best matching field type. The loop will only be used when no exact `FIELD_MAPPING` is found, so it should not hit the performance in general.

NOTE: The list of the base fields should be maintained separately in this case as the order matters (e.g. `Integer` should be tried before `Number`).

Another solution would be to introduce a field attribute, e.g. `swagger_type = ('integer', 'int32')`, so if the mapping fails, `_get_json_type_for_field` would look for that attribute before returing the default `('string', None)`.

The solutions can be combined to enable maximum customization.

For the immediate future, we plan to provide all the summary statistics you would find in SAV. This will be done in a data structure that provides that metrics for overall, by read and by lane per read. To do this, we first need to provide the ability to parse the run info.

The two metrics you asked for both depend on information that is only available in the RunInfo.xml. For example, first cycle intensity only reports the A channel or the Red channel. The order of the channels can only be extracted from the RunInfo. The example I provided will only consistently match SAV if you set up your own run info parser and extract the channel order and select the index of either the A channel or the Red channel.

Also, if you want %Q30 by read, then you need to know what a read is. This information is also only available in the RunInfo.xml.

The percent greater than Q30 is a little more complicated. You can try to do both of these yourself, but I wanted you to be aware that we do plan to do something more comprehensive in the future. I don't have a hard time line for this right now.

Usually, Z3 will try to detect the logic ([here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/portfolio/default_tactic.cpp)) and then run a specialized tactic for the problem. This is what happens when the `default` tactic is used (declared [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/portfolio/default_tactic.h#L29)). If none of those logic checks succeed, it will call the `smt` tactic ([here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/portfolio/default_tactic.cpp#L48)). On this particular problem, the `fpa2bv` and `bit-blaster` tactics are executed (among others) before handing the problem to the SAT solver. This is "eager", everything is translated into SAT and we use a SAT solver to decide the problem. 

However, when the `smt` tactic is used the approach is "lazy": nothing is done up front and only during the search, when things become relevant, they are translated (for the case of the FP logic, see [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/smt/theory_fpa.cpp)) or otherwise instantiated. This is what the "simple" solver does; it provides a direct path to the SMT solver without any tactics being involved. 

The solver obtained via `mk_solver` is a much more complicated one ([here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/portfolio/smt_strategic_solver.cpp)); it constructs a `combined_solver` that switches to the SMT solver if it sees incremental commands, otherwise it uses tactics (which may finally also result in running the SMT solver). Setting the verbosity to `-v:15` should print lines beginning with `(combined-solver ...` to tell you which one of them is used. 

Usually, we would expect that specialized tactics are better at solving their respective problems, but that's of course not always true. 

In this particular case, you should also see that Z3 switches to the SMT solver when one of the FP operations requires uninterpreted functions (e.g., `fp.to_ubv` when `hi_fp_unspecified=false`, default setting may change depending on upcoming modifications to the SMT FP standard). In this case, the goal will still contain uninterpreted functions after fpa2bv and bit-blaster have translated everything they can translate, and so we can't use the SAT solver. (In this case, that check is [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/fpa/qffp_tactic.cpp#L95) and there are similar ones in other tactics.)

A minor note: the "default" tactic tries to detect the logic _after_ running the simplifier (see [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/portfolio/default_tactic.cpp#L36)). So, when it doesn't detect a matching logic, it will run the "smt" tactic on the _simplified_ goal; so in these cases the actual tactic that gets executed is `(then simplify smt)` instead of just `smt`.

There are some different definitions of what it means to enumerate. 
It could be 

- with respect to all values of all variables.
- with respect to a subset of variables (others are treated as don't care).
- with respect to a subset of Boolean variables.

There are some good ways to enumerate models depending on the problem class. They don't generalize well across classes of problems. 
We haven't moved to support any of this as native APIs given the numerous possible ways to define such an enumeration.

@arranf Note if you're ever unsure why Z3 thinks something is unsatisfiable asking for it to give you the unsat core might help.

```
(set-option :produce-unsat-cores true)
(declare-const s String)
(declare-const x String)
(declare-const y Int)

(declare-const result String)

(declare-const slen Int)
(declare-const padlen Int)

(assert (!(= s "F") :named a0))
(assert (!(= x "_") :named a1))
(assert (!(= y 2) :named a2))
(assert (!(= padlen (str.len x)) :named a3))
(assert (!(= slen (str.len s)) :named a4))

(assert (!(= result s) :named a6))
(assert (! (forall ((i Int)) (implies ( and (>= i slen) (<= i y) ) (= (str.at result i) (str.at x (mod i padlen))))) :named a7) )

(check-sat)
(get-unsat-core)
```

Regarding issue 1 (fragmentation) I think this reflects that writing multiple datasets simultaneously is actually the wrong solution for the problem of storing data directly from acquisition systems into NWB stored in HDF5. It means breaking how HDF5 is designed to work well.

A better approach might be to store all raw acquisition data streamed into a single large dataset, so that chunking etc works naturally and efficiently. The data can then either (i) be moved once acquisition has finished into the NWB layout (which might give performance benefits for later processing, but is a potentially expensive one-off operation), or (ii) references used to link datasets in the NWB layout to the relevant hyperslabs in the raw acquisition dataset (a quick operation, but may require new features in the NWB schema, and may result in slower access to these individual datasets).

You are misunderstanding how ARM works. This is in thumb mode, where instructions can be only 16 bits long. Any program that tells you that the instruction bytes are `78 47 c0 46` is wrong. angr will not try to interpret this as an instruction either, our instruction decoding is done by libvex from the valgrind project. It will interpret `78 47` as bx pc, and then end the block. At least, I hope so. Your fix will definitely not work because nobody is trying to interpret `c0 48` as an instruction.

I don't know how to help you debug this. If you can provide any sort of testcase demonstrating this behavior, then I will be able to.

As you can see, heartbeat packets are coming both from the vehicle (srcSystem=1) and from the ground control station (srcSystem=255).

dronekit-python sees heartbeats from every source, and fails to map the Ground Control Station's current mode number to a mode string.

This is a problem with pymavlink - it doesn't let you lock onto a specific vehicle for input.

As also mentioned on the other ticket, I do have a solution for this situation, but I haven't managed to convince tridge of the concept.  You can have a look at the "avoidance" example (https://github.com/peterbarker/dronekit-python/tree/source-system-filtering/examples/avoidance) to see how you can use dronekit-python with multiple srcSystems floating around.

Ok, here is a updated version of the script. It tries to check each line and come up with a confidence index wether or not the line is a song title or not. 
Its based on a few factors, like does the line start with a number, does it look like a artist - track combination or does it have a time stamp.

Then it takes the lines with an index above average, which I feel works because tracklisting formatting is usually consistent so all lines should be equally above average, as long as there is one line or more weighing down the average. 

Once it has the list that it percieves as song titles, it tries to determine if the line has the artist in it or not. Checks wether or not the results are consistent, removes inconsistent results if they are few, or exits if it finds too many of them.

If it needs to find the artist name, it tries to parse the title by doing a youtube search on the title, then tries to repeatedly find the most frequent common string between a random subset of the results. Most of the time I tried this, it would only return the correct name, but sometimes it would have several results. (Like Best of, Album titles, etc), in the case of several alternatives, it will use the one that matches first in the original title. As I expect that the most common setup would be Artist name first.

> This was my first time using git rebase -i (I followed this excellent tutorial) so forgive me if I've messed something up. We'll see how the tests run.

FYI a quick glance at the tutorial that you post here seems to exclude the fixup and reword options. In the tutorial you post I see:
```
# Commands:
#  p, pick = use commit
#  e, edit = use commit, but stop for amending
#  s, squash = use commit, but meld into previous commit
```

There are two more commands (I guess depending on your git version):

```
#  r, reword = use commit, but edit the commit message
# f, fixup = like "squash", but discard this commit's log message
```

These are actually quite useful for rewording the commit message or just ignoring it if it's a simple "more fixes" type message. Arguably fixup is more useful than squash as you don't always want to keep every commit message.

The codecov failure is simply telling you that this PR has reduced the coverage overall by 0.01% and it's counting that as a 'failure'. This is completely ignorable and does not count against the PR in my opinion. The fact that the link takes you to a page with a passing tick is likely due to the fact that there's some small uncertainty in how it measures coverage and so sometimes it's coming up as -0.01% (fail) and sometimes as -0.00% (pass).

Secondly I should note that the coverage only counts the 'unit' tests' coverage so it doesn't account for the many integration tests we have.

I've pushed a commit (bbf9e73a43eb0d38eb982dea2dd94cf8f55511b2) which should fix this issue.

The problem is that our Video4Linux backend was not correctly determining which USB device corresponds to which /dev/video* file. It was only looking for a VID/PID match, which meant that multiple instances of the same device on Linux would be incorrectly associated. The new code matches based on USB bus number and device number, which should work regardless of number and combination of devices are plugged in.

`    rs2::frame_source& source) // Frame pool that can allocate new frames`
`  {`
`      rs2::frameset processed = align.process(data);`
`      rs2::video_frame other_frame = processed.first(align_to);`
`      rs2::depth_frame aligned_depth_frame = processed.get_depth_frame();`


`      if (!aligned_depth_frame || !other_frame)`
`      {`
`    return;`
`      }`

`      rs2::frame filtered = aligned_depth_frame; // Does not copy the frame, only adds a reference`

`      bool revert_disparity = false;`
`      for (auto&& filter : filters)`
`      {`
`    if (filter.is_enabled)`
`    {`
`        filtered = filter.filter.process(filtered);`

`      revert_disparity = true;`
`        }`
`    }`
`      }`
`      if (revert_disparity)`
`      {`
`    filtered = disparity_to_depth.process(filtered);`
`      }`

`      rs2::frameset combined = source.allocate_composite_frame({ filtered, other_frame });`
`      source.frame_ready(combined);`
`  });`

`  frame_processor >> filtered_data;`

`  while (!stopped) //While application is running`
`  {`
`      rs2::frameset fs;`
`      if (fs = pipe.wait_for_frames()) frame_processor.invoke(fs);`
`  }`

` });`

` while (app) {`
`  rs2::frameset frames = filtered_data.wait_for_frame();`
`  rs2::video_frame post_processed_image_frame = frames.get_color_frame();`
`  rs2::depth_frame post_processed_depth_frame = frames.get_depth_frame();`

`  if (post_processed_depth_frame && post_processed_image_frame)`
`  {`
`      float w = static_cast<float>(app.width());`
`      float h = static_cast<float>(app.height());`

`      rs2::video_frame color_frame = c(post_processed_depth_frame);`
`      rect altered_other_frame_rect{ 0, 0, w, h };`
`      altered_other_frame_rect = altered_other_frame_rect.adjust_ratio({ static_cast<float>(color_frame.get_width()),static_cast<float>(color_frame.get_height()) });`

`      renderer.render(color_frame, altered_other_frame_rect);`
`  }`
` }`

` stopped = true;`
` processing_thread.join();`

`    return EXIT_SUCCESS;`

I will only respond to the question of glue-tasks vs pass_job_info. Neither is what I was proposing, though pass_job_info *enables* it to be done. Yes, you can say well, pass_job_info will send the directory to the next Firework, and that Firework can do whatever it wants with it. To me, that is too much thinking and duplication involved. Say a static task precedes both a BS and Uniform Firework. I will have to code the specific handling of file copy (including logic of file formats, what files I should bring over, and what should not be brought over) into both the BS and Uniform Firework implementations. If for some reason the pass_job_info format gets changed, I will need to redo the implementations of BS, Uniform and many other places where this gets used.

My proposal is simply to treat files as a special data type that gets moved between Fireworks, with a proper language to specify what files are expected and provided by each FireWork. This is no different from the current input/output data, except that this file transfers data gets handled specially and transparently. I would say this solves 90% of the glue-task requirements in a much simpler framework. It is also a "glue" if you like that is used by *a lot* of use cases.

Nothing is preventing glue-tasks from continuing to exist on top of the above framework. In any case, I am a bit puzzled by the need for glue tasks in the first place. What is accomplished by glue tasks that is not accomplished by inserting an additional Firework? Let's say I have a static and BS run, and between those two runs, I want to do some data processing, what's stopping me from doing StaticFW->DataPRocessingFW->BSFW?

So basically, the file handling protocol that I propose is mainly to make Fireworks more user-friendly and simply a lot of atomate. If you have complex needs, glue tasks can continue to exist.
You should use ``add_additional_fields_to_taskdocs`` for this

The implementation for bispectrum components will likely be similar to the [BondOrientationalOrder](https://github.com/hackingmaterials/matminer/blob/25839c70e1747d97d763cde9aaaf03327ce602de/matminer/featurizers/site.py#L1773) class. The main hurdles to implementing it will be developing functions for the hyperspherical harmonics and coupling constants (`H`). I've already done some research on these:

- Hyperspherical harmonics can be expressed as [products of Legendre polynomials](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spherical_harmonics#Higher_dimensions), which are [implemented in scipy](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.special.lpmn.html). 
- The coupling constants (equation A.2 of the paper @athomps mentioned) are defined as products of Clebsch-Gordan Coefficients, which [are available in sympy](http://docs.sympy.org/latest/modules/physics/quantum/cg.html). Sympy is already a dependency of matminer and can be expensive, so you'll want to cache the results.

I'm not 100% on either of these suggestions but, hopefully, they give a good starting point. I hope to put some work in on this in the next few weeks, so let me know if anyone is interested in starting on this before me.

Hello @nisse3000 ,

This is an interesting proposal. For now, I'm in favor of keeping the pattern of always providing the information for `feature_labels`, `references`, and `implementators` in the function definition rather than the constructor to keep all of the featurizers with a consistent style.

We implemented each of these operations as functions so that their output could change depending on the current options for the featurizer. Generating these lists on-demand means that a user could change the options of the featurizer without re-instantiating it and prevents the feature labels from ever being out-of-sync with the current options. Of course, dynamically-generating the feature labels, implementors, or references is unnecessary for featurizers that lack options and adds a few lines of code (2?) per class. However, I worry that the advantages of the succinctness that your suggestion provides are offset by the added complexity of having some classes where the lists are implemented in the constructor and others where they are implemented in class methods.

Plus, on a minor note, I like keeping the operations in the constructor developed purely to setting the options of featurizer. Consistency concerns aside, assigning distinct functions of the featurizer to distinct class methods seems conceptually neater to me.

Are you convinced by these arguments? My concerns are almost purely aesthetic, and I don't have any technical objections to what you propose.

Thanks,
Logan

            # D loss

I think my biggest concern with this is that, we currently find ourselves running out of radio buffers during periods of heavy throughput.  Using a RadioPacket to report every ACK might further tax that resource.  Understand that the packet is received via an ISR but the RadioPacket is delivered via a task thread.  This means a pool of packets is needed so that the ISR can continue to function while the thread is delayed.

The models from [https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/english-vectors.html](https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/english-vectors.html) were trained on Wikipedia, as well as news data from the [WMT workshop](http://statmt.org/wmt16/translation-task.html) and the (UMBC corpora)[https://ebiquity.umbc.edu/resource/html/id/351/UMBC-webbase-corpus]. See #1128; that should fix it.

> I like the implementation via cast since it is extremely simple, and it also makes it possible to write thing like `auto str = py::cast<const std::string &>(object);`

The single parameter is workable, but I'm not sure this belongs in the public `cast` method: I can't see any way that `auto str = py::cast<const std::string &>(object);` is useful: it's going to be *less* efficient¹ than `auto str = py::cast<std::string>(object)`, plus has side effects in invalidating any other std::string references.  Getting a static_assert with `py::cast<ref_type>` is a nice reminder that you really can't rely on reference types to value converted types working anything like they do in C++.  And since we're going to need a seperate method (or at least specialization) anyway, it seems cleaner to hide this away into some dedicated `detail::virtual_cast` that just calls `py::cast()` wherever that works, and does the local cache storage and return for a reference or pointer type.

Another way to look at it: what we're doing here (returning a reference to a local variable) is pretty gross, and a pretty big violation of the whole point of reference/pointers.  If it wasn't a necessary evil, we wouldn't be doing it at all, but let's keep that evilness as limited as possible.

¹ less efficient because the non-ref version should be able to use RVO to just do one copy (from the python string into the eventual `std::string`), while the ref version has to do that *and* at least a move (or worse, a copy, depending on the type) into the static local.

The solution in e4fb05ab494d531331423111f94e5bbb430b67c5 will not work for overload sets which have a variable number of arguments, e.g. `void foo()` and `void foo(int)`. That's the main reason why the two stage template parameters are needed (class template parameters + static member template parameters). See the `test.cpp` file in https://github.com/dean0x7d/overload_cast for the relevant tests.

The `std::false_type` argument is there to allow programmatic selection of `const` vs non-`const` overloads, based on some type trait. E.g. `overload_cast<int>(&W::foo, std::is_same<T, U>{})`. It's not needed for manual casting, but it's pretty nice for metaprogramming.

It's really up to you how you want to wrap it; there's nothing special about the functions being in a namespace.

You can bind them as regular functions with `m.def("python_func_name", &namespace::c_func_name);` or you could create a `py::class_` and bind them as static class methods using `cl.def_static("python_static_method_name", &namespace::c_func_name)`.

```c++
BogusImplicitConversion(const BogusImplicitConversion &) {}

@SwartzCr,

If I'm correctly understanding the issue, it looks like, the `plugins_cmd` must be called before the call to `make_or_verify_needed_dirs(config)` in `certbot.main.main` function. Where would be good place to do this?

The current assumption is accounts are not shared between servers at different URLs which seems like a safe and usually correct assumption, however, boulder implemented the ability to share accounts between ACME versions. (This is the case in production and probably the case in staging too but we should double check the latter.) I think we should continue to make the assumption accounts aren't shared in general and add a special case for Let's Encrypt. Normally I'm against hard coding Let's Encrypt's policies like this and having an ancient version of Certbot come back to bite us later, but this isn't likely to change and allowing the account to be shared is a significant benefit for us, Let's Encrypt, and our users.

The relevant class is `certbot.account.AccountFileStorage`, specifically it's usage of `config.accounts_dir`, and especially the `find_all` method. The way I'd do this is to make a data structure containing the server URLs across which accounts can be shared in `accounts.py`, `constants.py`, or `configuration.py`. When `find_all` is called, it can use these to check the account directories for the other endpoints to see what other accounts exist.

I think the big question here is whether we always check these other directories or only if an account doesn't exist in the account directory specifically for that endpoint. The reason for considering the latter is to avoid adding new prompts to Certbot.

If you look at `certbot.main._determine_account.py`, we will prompt if there is more than one matching account. Since people have been changing the server URL to use ACMEv2 and we switched ACMEv2 to the default staging URL, I think this will cause problems. For instance, I think it's fairly common that a user has an ACMEv1 prod account and a staging account for both endpoints. If we make the change to have `find_all` always return both accounts, if you run `certbot renew --dry-run` I'd expect Certbot to crash saying it needs you to choose an account but can't do so because `renew` implies non-interactive :/ Similar problems would happen for people who have already started using ACMEv2 in prod, have a ACMEv1 account, and are using the `--non-interactive` flag.

The other piece of this that I haven't fully thought through is if we use an account from a directory other than the one matching the server URL being used, how do we find that account during renewal? Unfortunately, I think we have to break forwards compatibility here as I don't see a way to make this work with an older version of Certbot. If we're OK with that, the two options I thought of are to add the account directory used to the runtime config and save it in the renewal conf file or to reuse the logic from `find_all` to search other account directories in `certbot.account.AccountFileStorage.load`. The latter seems much easier and less error prone to me.

If you're still with me, let me know if you have any questions about all this. Happy to answer them, talk design, or try to take this issue entirely if prefer.

That's great! I'm glad it's going well and thanks for working on it.

> By switching all internal data to unicode, it's possible that third party plugins will get broken. Do you think certbot needs a transition plan? Or is it OK to force all plugins to use from __future__ import unicode_literals since some point?

Another good point. We certainly want to avoid breakage for 3rd party plugins. I'm open to ideas on how to best accomplish this. We could maybe handle the conversion for them and if they return a non-Unicode string we just convert it, but there also the opposite problem of us suddenly giving them Unicode which may cause issues.

Unless you see a reasonable path forward there (or somewhere else), I think the best approach is to work with them towards this change. We maintain a list of most 3rd party plugins that we are aware of [here](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#third-party-plugins). We can reach out to them to make sure they are aware of what we're doing and help them through the process.

I do think it's reasonable to say after point X we will be making this change, but we should do everything we can to make sure 3rd party plugins are updated before that time comes.

> But a regular python programmer is used to PEP8, and it is hard to shake off, especially when most of the python libraries (including TF & pytorch) follow this style to some extent. So there will be a constant tension, that I'd like to minimize.

Since I deeply dislike PEP8 and the entire culture around bike-shedding and lack of open-mindedness in much of the python community, I think this tension might be hard to avoid ;)

> I meant configuring linters to return warnings that you truly want to fix. 

Yup. Although I haven't found any linters that are thoughtful enough to actually work the way I want for nearly any language, unfortunately.

> To create a consistent style we can see what types of warnings we have in the code and decide what to correct and which checks to disable:

Thanks this is helpful

>   3110 E231 missing whitespace after ','

This is intentional. Nearly every class has something like:

    self.x,self.y = x,y

The spacing clearly separates LHS from RHS. Adding a space after commas here makes it less readable (to me).

>   1938 W191 indentation contains tabs

That is not intentional. Where do we have tabs? I thought my vimrc automatically fixes that.

>   669 E501 line too long (83 > 79 characters)

The 80 char line rule is a hangover from a long-gone era!

>   266 E302 expected 2 blank lines, found 1

Many classes are just a line or two, and often they're put right next to each other so you can clearly see how they are similar/different. Only classes longer than a about screen should have 2 blank lines.

>   188 E701 multiple statements on one line (colon)

I much prefer this:

```
if a: do_a()
else: do_b()
```

Generally, where the 2 parts of a conditional or try block fit on a line and don't have too much going on, I prefer to have them together.

>   164 E225 missing whitespaces around operator

For each operator it depends on the context. Generally if there aren't spaces around '=' it's probably a mistake. But for math equations I try to lay it out closest to how it looks in a paper. Often that means no spaces around some operators.

>   142 E128 continuation line under-indented for visual indent

Thanks for the example. Replacing 2 lines of code here with 3 means more vertical space is used for no (IMO) good reason, so I'd rather not do that.

>   130 E111 indentation is not a multiple of four

Generally I try to lay things out in a way that maximizes ability to understand the code block with jumping around. I'm sure there are examples where I've done a suboptimal job and am happy to fix them on a case by case basis.

> Here is a corrected version of PEP8 code. I hope it isn't that bad anymore.

It's better, although I still don't like the conditional.

> Let me know which PEP8 checks would you like to disable. Does the list I've suggested make sense?

I can't see any checks here which could be automated without breaking things, except perhaps for the tabs (I'd want to see what was triggering that first though).

Thanks again for your careful analysis!

@nsmith1024 wrote: 

> If i save the stream using "-o file.mp4" it saves the stream data into that file

... When you manually specify an ".mp4" file extension, that doesn't mean that 
streamlink will use the MP4 container, because streamlink doesn't do any remuxing 
by default; as beardypig said, 

> the format is the same as the original stream, and the same data goes to VLC as goes to a file.  

If you install MediaInfo (`https://mediaarea.net/en/MediaInfo`) you can then inspect, say, a 5min stream capture to discover its details (raw streams + media container).

> I can double click on that file and it plays in windows

That is because it so happens the media player associated with MP4 files on your Windows setup can also handle the MPEG-TS container (and video/audio codecs inside it...). 

> is there a program or programs, that can read the stdout of streamlink, 
> and transform that to mp4 or ogg on the fly so it can be streamed to mp4 or ogg player? 

I don't want to step into beardypig's shoes, but: 

A local MP4 file (playable by your custom player) is a different thing to an MP4 file streamed to your custom player:
 In order for an MP4 file to become streamable, it must have its moov atom placed in the start of the file; 
I can imagine using ffmpeg to remux the AppleHLS MPEG-TS stream losslessly to the MP4 container, 
but making it streamable requires a second pass with the `moveflags faststart` switch, also unsure if that is even possible with live streams. 

As for the OGG container, I'm afraid it may be incompatible with the audio/video codecs used natively in twitch.tv streams; you may have to implement transcoding for both the video (to theora?) and audio (to ogg vorbis?) for that to work, which is a resource hog on the streaming machine...

> I expect the current time and duration of an HLS stream to be shown properly
> This occurs with and without --player-passthrough hls

Using and not using the player-passthrough parameter is a huge difference in how streamlink will work.

When you're using the parameter with the `hls` value, Streamlink will just try to get the HLS URL from the input source and will use the extracted HLS URL as launch parameter of your player. This means that the player will have to do all of the HLS work on its own and therefore should be able to let you seek within the video and also tell you its overall duration if it is not a live stream. Since Streamlink doesn't do anything in this case, it's up to your selected player to support this.

If you're not using the player-passthrough parameter, which is the default, Streamlink will do the HLS (or various other streaming protocols) work on its own, which means that it will download the playlists and segments and will turn them into a single progressive stream which is then being piped into the stdin channel of the spawned player process in the default configuration. This further means that you're not able to seek in the output stream and also won't know its overall duration. See #134

For generating a unique version tag, see for example https://github.com/spirit-code/spirit/tree/master/core/python
```Python
def get_git_commit_datetime():
    try:
        commit_hash = subprocess.check_output("git rev-parse HEAD", shell=True, stderr=subprocess.STDOUT).decode("utf-8").strip()
        commit_datetime = subprocess.check_output("git show -s --format=%ci "+commit_hash, shell=True, stderr=subprocess.STDOUT).decode("utf-8").strip()
        print(commit_datetime)
        datetime_object = datetime.datetime.strptime(commit_datetime, '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S +%f')
        print("{:%Y%m%d%H%M%S}".format(datetime_object))
        return "{:%Y%m%d%H%M%S}".format(datetime_object)
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError as cpe:
        print(cpe.output)
        return "00000000000000"
...
    # If the environment variable SPIRIT_VERSION_SUFFIX is defined,
    # it is appended to the package version number.
    version_suffix = ""
    add_version_suffix = os.environ.get("SPIRIT_ADD_VERSION_SUFFIX", "")
    if add_version_suffix.lower() in ("yes", "true", "t", "1"):
        timepoint_string = get_git_commit_datetime()
        if timepoint_string == "00000000000000":
            timepoint_string = "{:%Y%m%d%H%M}".format(datetime.datetime.now())
        version_suffix = ".dev"+timepoint_string
        print("setup.py: package version suffix = ", version_suffix)
```
The environment variable is used to optionally add a `.dev<timepoint>` string, while otherwise the base version of the package is used. That means the version is set manually and not read from git tags.

The idea is to mainly work on a `develop` branch and only merge releases (i.e. a new version number) to the master branch, where the `.dev` tag would not be added by the CI.

Note for posterity:

One of the basic design questions here is whether we want to support native async I/O primitives when they're available, or whether we want to commit to using threads for everything all the time. This makes a big difference, because using native async I/O primitives requires pretty much throwing out the stdlib `io` module entirely, and then if we have to write something from scratch there's a question of whether to try and implement the same interfaces or else do something different.

The options are: Windows has some async file I/O options (basically just `pread`/`pwrite`, not like `stat` or `open` or anything), and Linux has some extremely limited async file I/O options that are almost never useful in practice (in particular they require disabling the buffer cache). Who knows, maybe someday it will get better but [I'm not holding my breath](https://lwn.net/Articles/671649/).

Microsoft says that "async" file I/O sometimes blocks: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/help/156932/asynchronous-disk-i-o-appears-as-synchronous-on-windows ([a 2014 comment reporting the same thing](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/25656171/windows-overlapped-io-vs-io-on-separate-thread#comment40093309_25656171)). Supposedly the same is true for Linux's native AIO (I think what happens is the actual block writing can be async, but stuff like walking the extent tree to figure out where the blocks will go is still synchronous – basically Linux's AIO is really only intended for use on raw block devices by the kind of mini-operating system that masquerades as an RDBMS).

In addition, various reports suggest that doing synchronous I/O from a thread-pool is actually faster than doing native async I/O. There's nothing magic about kernel async I/O implementations; a kernel thread and a kernel state machine are essentially the same thing, plus the synchronous paths get *way* more attention from maintainers. (You may have heard that internally, the Windows kernel is all async, so async operations will always be just as fast as synchronous ones. It turns out that this isn't true – they have a [special fast path](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/drivers/ifs/irps-are-different-from-fast-i-o) for synchronous I/O on ordinary files!) User space threads are a bit more expensive than kernel threads, but with an efficient thread pool it doesn't make much difference. Probably the main benefit that native kernel support could potentially provide is that the kernel can detect when the data being requested is already in RAM, and return it immediately without any kind of thread overhead at all. But in practice there aren't any actually-usable APIs that reliably work like this.

Presumably for these reasons, libuv uses a thread pool for disk I/O in all configurations, and this seems to be universally agreed to be the right solution when writing C programs.

Does trio being written in Python make a difference? Well, our thread synchronization overhead is much (much) worse than a well-tuned C thread pool. Right now it's especially silly because we actually spawn a new thread for every operation instead of caching them like a thread pool does, but then, the reason we do that is that finding a thread in the cache and scheduling a job to it is expensive enough from Python that spawning a new thread each time is nearly as good. In any case, we can certainly improve `run_in_worker_thread` if we have to, but between the GIL and general Python overhead (including e.g. trio's rescheduling logic) it's never going to do a single syscall dispatch as fast as a tight C implementation. Also, things like buffered read/writes are especially silly here, because we may end up going into a new thread just to read some bytes out of a userspace buffer that's right in front of us. Doh.

OTOH, the stdlib `io` module is all written in C (for CPython); if we reimplemented the stack ourselves to reduce the thread overhead, then our reimplementation would itself add some overhead.

In conclusion, I think going all-in on `run_in_worker_thread` is a reasonable idea. It's not at all clear that anything better is possible, and while it's not trivial (see all the discussion in #180), it's **much** simpler than defining a whole new file I/O stack from scratch. And worst case, we can eventually implement our own duck-compatible objects if we have to and the benefit is there (i.e., have `wrap_file` magically detect and return some special optimized object if it's given an object representing a vanilla on-disk file).
